name: La'ie Point State Wayside
tagline: A dramatic windswept cliff with views of a natural sea arch and offshore bird sanctuary — free, quick, and totally uncrowded.
parking: Only about 10 spots at the end of Naupaka St — park further down and walk if full. Be respectful of residents
bestTime: Late afternoon for golden light on the cliffs. Morning for calmer seas and better visibility of Kukuihoolua Island
talkStory: Hawaiian legend says Kukuihoolua Island -- the one with the natural arch punched through it -- was formed when the demigod Kana broke a giant lizard (mo'o) into five pieces, creating the offshore islets. The point is sacred in Hawaiian culture and was once used for celestial navigation. Today it's a seabird sanctuary, and the dramatic cliffs are among the most photographed on the Windward Coast yet rarely crowded because most tourists never leave the nearby Polynesian Cultural Center.
